background.text:
BACKGROUND
The San Francisco South East Treatment Plant has applied for an Authority to Construct and/or 
Permit to Operate for the following: 
S-(110) Pretreatment Operations [existing] 
abated by 
A-(110) Biofiltration Odor Control, four vessels [new] 
91,000 cfm to A-111 in normal mode, 60,667 cfm to A-111 and 30,333 cfm to 
atmosphere in bypass mode, 91,000 cfm to atmosphere in alternative normal 
mode 
and 
A-(111) Carbon Adsorption Odor Control, three vessels [new]  
91,000 cfm to atmosphere in normal mode, 60,667 in bypass mode 
In normal mode, A-110 and A-111 are in series - 91,000 cfm will pass through A-110 before 
exhausting from A-111. In bypass mode, the flow rate will stay the same, but 30,333 cfm will 
exhaust from A-110 and the remaining 60,667 cfm will exhaust from A-111. In alternative 
normal mode, 91,000 cfm will exhaust directly from A-110. 
The following abatement devices will be removed: 
A-(1204) Biofilter, 2,500 cfm 
A-(1205) Biofilter, 2,500 cfm 
And an unpermitted dual (dry) media scrubber, 18,000 cfm 
The sources above will be located at 750 Phelps Street, San Francisco, CA 94124. 
The San Francisco South East Treatment Plant (SEP), owns and operates a municipal wastewater 
treatment plant. This plant was constructed in 1952 and handles about 80% of San Francisco’s 
wastewater from the eastern two thirds of the city’s residents. SEP’s peak wet weather flow rate 
is approximately 250 million gallons per day (MGD); 150 MGD receives primary and secondary 
treatment and the additional 100 MGD receives only primary treatment. The permitted average 
dry weather flow rate is 85.4 million gallons per day. This project will permit the new 
preliminary treatment facility (SEP 020 Headworks), which will replace the existing headworks 
buildings SEP 011 (wet weather headworks) and SEP 012 (all weather headworks) under Source 
110, Preliminary Treatment.

(9/03/2019), and A-7005, Digester Gas Flare, has been permitted under application 29047 
(5/29/2018). A-7005 has since been shut down and will not be included. The table below showsthe toxic emissions from the related applications:

The HRA estimates residential risk assuming exposure to annual average TAC concentrations 
occurring 350 days per year, for 30 years. Risk estimate for offsite workers assumes an exposure 
that occurs 8 hours per day, 250 days per year, for 25 years. 
 
Results from this HRA indicate that the maximum project cancer risk is estimated at 0.90 in a 
million, that the project chronic hazard index is 0.086, and that the project acute health index is 
1.1. In accordance with the District’s Regulation 2-5-301, TBACT is not required because each 
individual source risk does not exceed a cancer risk of 1.0 in a million and/or a chronic health 
index of 0.20. The estimated project cancer risk does not exceed 6.0 in a million and the project 
chronic health index does not exceed 1.0, but the project acute health index exceeds 1.0, so the 
project does not comply with the District’s Regulation 2-5-302 project risk requirements.  
 
To reduce the acute health index to no more than 1.0, the maximum hourly emissions of H 2S 
from S-110 must be limited to no more than 0.41 pounds during bypass operation. Modeling 
done to confirm compliance with Regulation 9-2-301 found that H 2S hourly emissions must be 
no more than 0.37 lb/hr during normal operation. Therefore, H 2S emissions will be limited to 
0.76 ppm:from A-110’s bypass stack, P-109). As described above in the Toxic Risk Screening Section, this
is expected to benefit air dispersion as the stack velocity at P-109 will increase compared to 
bypass mode operation for maintenance.

CEQA.narrative:
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA)
) 
This project, consisting of the proposed changes to Pretreatment Operations, S-110, was the 
subject of San Francisco Planning Department Case File No. 2015-006224ENV, for which an 
Initial Study/Mitigated Negative Declaration was conducted. The initial Mitigated Negative 
Declaration (MND) was approved by the lead agency (City and County of San Francisco) on 
February 28, 2017 in San Francisco Public Utilities Commission (SFPUC) Resolution No. 17-
0043. The project was later updated, with the Amended Mitigated Negative Declaration being 
approved by the City and County of San Francisco on October 8, 2019 in SFPUC Resolution No. 
19-0195.  
 
This permit application was subject to a Mitigated Negative Declaration prepared by an agency 
other than the Air District. Prior to approving the permit application, the Air District reviewed 
and considered the Negative Declaration adopted by the lead agency, including public 
comments. 
 
California Health & Safety Code §42301.6 and Regulation 2-1-412 Pursuant to California Health & Safety Code §42301.6(a), prior to approving an application for a 
permit to construct or modification of a source, which is located within 1,000 feet from the outer 
boundary of a school site, the District shall prepare a public notice as detailed in §42301.6.  
§42301.9(a) defines a “school” as any public or private school used for the purposes of the 
education of more than 12 children in kindergarten or any grades 1 to 12, inclusive, but does not 
include any private school in which education is primarily conducted in private homes. 
 
Using the GreatSchools.org website and searching with Google Maps, it has been determined 
that the source will not be located within 1,000 feet of the outer boundary of any K-12 school 
site.  Therefore, the project is not subject to the public notice requirements due to proximity to a 
school.

91,000 cfm to atmosphere in normal mode, 60,667 in bypass mode

A-(1204) Biofilter, 2,500 cfm 
A-(1205) Biofilter, 2,500 cfm

The San Francisco South East Treatment Plant (SEP), owns and operates a municipal wastewater

blocks.heading: DRAFT
blocks.text:
DRAFT
Plant No. 568 (San Francisco South East Treatment Plant)
  Application No. 30200 
Page 2 
 Pretreatment Operations, S-110 
 
The new preliminary treatment facility, SEP 020 Headworks, will be enclosed and will include 
fine screens, grit tanks with grit washers, and an odor control system. The proposed changes will 
not affect the wastewater flow at this facility. 
 
Odor control system: A-110, Biofiltration Odor Control, and A-111, Carbon Adsorption Odor 
Control  
 
The new odor control system abating odors from SEP 020 Headworks will be a two-step system 
consisting of four biotrickling filter vessels followed by three activated carbon adsorption 
vessels. Three fans (one redundant, each fan rated for 45,500 cfm) will serve the odor control 
system. Each individual biotrickling filter vessel and carbon vessel will have a design capacity of 
30,333 cfm. The total system will have a design capacity of 91,000 cfm of foul air.  
 
During normal operation, foul air will first pass through the biotrickling filters (A-110) before 
exhausting out of each of the three carbon adsorber stacks (A-111).  
 
The system will have a bypass mode, during which 30,333 cfm of treated foul air will exhaust 
out of the single biotrickling filter exhaust stack (bypass stack, P-109) without passing through a 
carbon adsorber. The other 60,667 cfm of treated foul air will exhaust out of two of the carbon 
adsorber stacks after passing through the biotrickling filters. Bypass mode will allow the third 
carbon adsorber to be taken down for maintenance. The facility estimates that replacement of the 
carbon media on one vessel will take 150 hours, so operation in bypass mode will not exceed 500 
hours/year total for the maintenance of the three carbon adsorption vessels. The facility has 
requested more flexibility in the permit condition for their hours of bypass mode operation for 
maintenance. While a given biotrickling filter vessel is only expected to be taken down for 
maintenance every 10 years, more maintenance time may be needed when a biotrickling filter 
vessel and carbon vessel must be maintained in the same year. The facility has been allowed to 
have 500 hours of maintenance per biotrickling or carbon vessel because the risk and air 
dispersion modeling show that they will not be adversely affected by this change, as explained in 
more depth in the respective sections below. A-110 and A-111 will be held to the same limits 
during bypass mode as during normal mode. 
 
The facility has also requested to be able to operate in an alternative normal mode, in which the 
system will emit from P-109 during normal operation so that the life of the carbon adsorption 
vessels can be preserved. During alternative normal operation, the odor control system will emit 
91,000 cfm from P-109 without first passing through A-111. A-110 will be held to the same 
limits during alternative normal mode as during normal mode.     
 
The facility proposed an H 2S outlet concentration limit of 1 ppm. However, HRA and air 
dispersion analysis results found that an H 2S outlet concentration not exceeding 0.76 ppm would 
allow the project to comply with Regulation 2, Rule 5 and Regulation 9, Rule 2. Therefore, the 
odor control system will have an outlet H 2S concentration limit of 0.76 ppm. An outlet ammonia 
limit of 1 ppm will also be imposed on the odor control system.

 The facility proposed a VOC outlet concentration limit of 20 ppm. However, the odor control 
system is expected to emit more than 10 lb/day of VOC at that outlet concentration. VOC BACT 
for POTW odor control devices has been determined to be an outlet concentration of 10 ppm 
VOC, measured as methane, which will be imposed as a limit.    
 
The existing odor control system for Pretreatment Operations, S-110, consists of a dry media 
scrubber treating 18,000 cfm of foul air from the SEP 012 process area and two sea-shell 
biofilters, A-1204 and A-1205, treating a total of 5,000 cfm of foul air from the SEP 012 
distribution chambers and channel.   
 
 
EMISSIONS CALCULATIONS The new pretreatment facility, SEP 020 Headworks, will be enclosed. All emissions will be 
routed through A-110 and A-111, which are expected to remove 99% and 99.5% of inlet H 2S, 
respectively. However, the facility has asked for a limit of 1 ppm H 2S at the outlet and will 
receive a limit of 0.76 ppm to comply with Regulation 2, Rule 5 and Regulation 9, Rule 2. This 
limit is enforced to ensure that odors from the source are adequately controlled. Calculations 
were made using the Ideal Gas Law and assuming 1 ppmv H 2S and standard conditions at the 
abatement device outlets.  
 
Normal operation 
 
Normal operation of the new odor control system will take place whenever there are no carbon 
adsorption vessels being taken down for maintenance. In normal operation, foul air from S-110 
will exit through the three stacks associated with A-111 at a combined 91,000 cfm after passing 
through A-110. As bypass operation will occur for approximately 500 hours per year, the system 
will be in normal operation for approximately 8,260 hours per year.

operates in alternative normal mode.

adsorption vessel needs to be taken down for maintenance. In bypass operation, 30,333 cfm of

passing through A-110. Bypass operation is expected to occur for a maximum of 500 hours per 
year.

The HRA estimates residential risk assuming exposure to annual average TAC concentrations 
occurring 350 days per year, for 30 years. Risk estimate for offsite workers assumes an exposure 
that occurs 8 hours per day, 250 days per year, for 25 years. 
 
Results from this HRA indicate that the maximum project cancer risk is estimated at 0.90 in a 
million, that the project chronic hazard index is 0.086, and that the project acute health index is 
1.1. In accordance with the District’s Regulation 2-5-301, TBACT is not required because each 
individual source risk does not exceed a cancer risk of 1.0 in a million and/or a chronic health 
index of 0.20. The estimated project cancer risk does not exceed 6.0 in a million and the project 
chronic health index does not exceed 1.0, but the project acute health index exceeds 1.0, so the 
project does not comply with the District’s Regulation 2-5-302 project risk requirements.  
 
To reduce the acute health index to no more than 1.0, the maximum hourly emissions of H 2S 
from S-110 must be limited to no more than 0.41 pounds during bypass operation. Modeling 
done to confirm compliance with Regulation 9-2-301 found that H 2S hourly emissions must be 
no more than 0.37 lb/hr during normal operation. Therefore, H 2S emissions will be limited to 
0.76 ppm:

Different from the assumptions used for the HRA, the facility will be allowed to operate in 
alternative normal mode (all flow, 91,000 cfm, emitted from the bypass stack, P-109). The 
facility has also been allowed to increase the allowed maintenance hours from 500 hrs/yr to 500 
hours per year per carbon or biotrickling filter vessel. Neither of these changes are expected to 
have a detrimental effect on the risk analysis or air dispersion analysis.  
 
As described above, the lowest limit of 0.37 lb/hr H 2S was due to emissions during normal 
operation. Therefore, an increase in bypass mode operation for maintenance is not expected to 
increase the health risk.      
 
In addition, alternative normal operation will not increase emissions over normal operation and 
will increase the stack velocity at the bypass stack, P-109. The emission limits will remain the 
same and the flow rate through P-109 will increase from 30,333 cfm (during bypass mode for 
maintenance) to 91,000 cfm during alternative normal mode. This would increase dispersion of 
the treated foul air and lower the fence line H 2S concentration and health risk compared to the 
modeled scenarios.

equal 10.0 lbs or greater of any BACT pollutant. The increase in emissions due to the

plants, including application 29792 (for Anchor Brewing, plant 1446), application 29889 (for 
City of Richmond WPCD, plant 2482), and application 29524 (for Sewerage Agency of 
Southern Marin, plant 1345). BACT for POC was established as limiting organic emissions from 
devices abating wastewater treatment sources to 10 ppm, measured as methane. 
 
A-110 and A-111 are going to have an organic emissions limit of 10 ppm, measured as methane, 
and will therefore satisfy BACT for POC.  
 
 
OFFSETS Emission offset requirements for POC and NOx are set out in Regulation 2, Rule 2, Section 302. 
Per a District policy, “Calculating Potential to Emit for Emergency Backup Power Generators”, 
signed on 6/3/2019, 100 hours of emergency operation will be assumed, in addition to the 50 
hours allowed for reliability-related and testing operation, for the purpose of determining the 
applicability of District permitting regulations. Therefore, this policy will be used in the 
calculation of the PTE of emergency backup power generators. POC and NOx offsets are 
required for new or modified sources at a facility that emits or will be permitted to emit 10 tons 
per year or more of that pollutant. The PTE for this facility is over 10 tpy for NOx and POC, so 
offsets are required. To date, offsets have been provided by the Small Facilities Bank. 
 
An analysis produced for the facility’s last application, #30297, in 2020, showed that the facility 
had the potential to emit 32.622 tons per year of NOx and 28.862 tons per year of POC.  
 
In this application, the facility will be changing the PTE by removing the existing headworks 
odor control system (A-1204, A1205) and installing the new odor control system abating SEP 
020 Headworks. The new PTE for NOx and POC would be 32.622 and 36.278 tons per year, 
respectively. The facility has agreed to lower the limit on the POC emission factor for S-10, 
Cogeneration Engine, in permit condition 17161, part 3 from 0.6 g POC/bhp-hr to 0.45 g/bhp-hr. 
This will lower the PTE for POC to 32.034 tons per year. The facility can continue to use offsets 
from the Small Facility Banking Account (SFBA) for NOx and POC. 
 
The offsets requirements for PM 10, PM2.5, and SOx are specified in Regulation 2, Rule 2, Section 
303. Per Section 303, PM 10, PM2.5, and SOx emission offsets are required for any new or 
modified source that is a major facility for PM 10, PM2.5, or SOx emissions. SFPUC is not a major 
facility for PM 10, PM2.5, and SOx emissions. Therefore, offsets for PM 10, PM2.5, and SOx are not 
required for this application.  
 
Offsets are not required for CO. 
 
The potential to emit calculations are in the file.

Regulation 2, Rule 2: Prevention of Significant Deterioration (PSD) Requirement 
The PSD requirements in District Regulation 2, Rule 2, Section 304 and 305 apply to major 
modifications at a major facility. This site is not a major facility. Therefore, Regulation 2-2-304 
and 2-2-305 do not apply. 
 
Regulation 2, Rule 6: Major Facility Review 
This rule requires that major and designated facilities apply for a Title V federal operating permit. 
This facility is not a major facility. 
 
Regulation 3: Fees 
The facility has paid the application fees billed under Invoice 4GN38. 
 
Regulation 9, Rule 2: Inorganic Gaseous Pollutants: Hydrogen Sulfide This rule limits ground level concentrations of hydrogen sulfide to 0.06 ppm averaged over three 
consecutive minutes or 0.03 ppm averaged over any 60 consecutive minutes. On November 16, 
2023, the Air District modeled H 2S emissions to determine whether S-110 (Pretreatment 
Operations) will comply with the Rule due to emissions from A-110 and A-111 (Biofiltration 
Odor Control and Carbon Adsorption Odor Control). A-110 has four vessels and can emit from 
one stack (to be used during bypass mode) and A-111 has three vessels and three stacks. The 
stack outlet at A-110 is 72.00” in diameter and 48.00” at A-111. Each stack is capable of 
releasing 30,333 cfm, for a total maximum flow of 91,000 cfm from A-110 and A-111. The 
facility requested that emissions from the abatement devices be limited by permit condition to 
1.0 part per million, which results in H 2S emissions of 0.481 lb/hr. According to the modeled 
H2S emissions, the Air District determined that the facility would comply with Regulation 9, 
Rule 2 with stack outlet heights of 33.25 feet above ground level and H 2S emissions of 0.37 
lb/hr. At the stack outlet of 33.25 feet, the Air District determined the 1-hour average 
concentration of H 2S to be less than 37.81 ug/m3 = 0.03 ppm at this emissions level. The model 
assumed that the H 2S concentration will not exceed 1.0 ppm. A limit of 0.76 ppm H 2S, which the 
facility has agreed to, will reduce emissions to 0.37 lb/hr. Therefore, S-110 will be in

California Health & Safety Code §42301.6 and Regulation 2-1-412 Pursuant to California Health & Safety Code §42301.6(a), prior to approving an application for a 
permit to construct or modification of a source, which is located within 1,000 feet from the outer 
boundary of a school site, the District shall prepare a public notice as detailed in §42301.6.  
§42301.9(a) defines a “school” as any public or private school used for the purposes of the

 Pretreatment Operations, S-110 
 
The new preliminary treatment facility, SEP 020 Headworks, will be enclosed and will include 
fine screens, grit tanks with grit washers, and an odor control system. The proposed changes will 
not affect the wastewater flow at this facility. 
 
Odor control system: A-110, Biofiltration Odor Control, and A-111, Carbon Adsorption Odor 
Control  
 
The new odor control system abating odors from SEP 020 Headworks will be a two-step system 
consisting of four biotrickling filter vessels followed by three activated carbon adsorption 
vessels. Three fans (one redundant, each fan rated for 45,500 cfm) will serve the odor control 
system. Each individual biotrickling filter vessel and carbon vessel will have a design capacity of 
30,333 cfm. The total system will have a design capacity of 91,000 cfm of foul air.  
 
During normal operation, foul air will first pass through the biotrickling filters (A-110) before 
exhausting out of each of the three carbon adsorber stacks (A-111).  
 
The system will have a bypass mode, during which 30,333 cfm of treated foul air will exhaust 
out of the single biotrickling filter exhaust stack (bypass stack, P-109) without passing through a 
carbon adsorber. The other 60,667 cfm of treated foul air will exhaust out of two of the carbon 
adsorber stacks after passing through the biotrickling filters. Bypass mode will allow the third 
carbon adsorber to be taken down for maintenance. The facility estimates that replacement of the 
carbon media on one vessel will take 150 hours, so operation in bypass mode will not exceed 500 
hours/year total for the maintenance of the three carbon adsorption vessels. The facility has 
requested more flexibility in the permit condition for their hours of bypass mode operation for 
maintenance. While a given biotrickling filter vessel is only expected to be taken down for 
maintenance every 10 years, more maintenance time may be needed when a biotrickling filter 
vessel and carbon vessel must be maintained in the same year. The facility has been allowed to 
have 500 hours of maintenance per biotrickling or carbon vessel because the risk and air 
dispersion modeling show that they will not be adversely affected by this change, as explained in 
more depth in the respective sections below. A-110 and A-111 will be held to the same limits 
during bypass mode as during normal mode. 
 
The facility has also requested to be able to operate in an alternative normal mode, in which the 
system will emit from P-109 during normal operation so that the life of the carbon adsorption 
vessels can be preserved. During alternative normal operation, the odor control system will emit 
91,000 cfm from P-109 without first passing through A-111. A-110 will be held to the same 
limits during alternative normal mode as during normal mode.     
 
The facility proposed an H 2S outlet concentration limit of 1 ppm. However, HRA and air 
dispersion analysis results found that an H 2S outlet concentration not exceeding 0.76 ppm would 
allow the project to comply with Regulation 2, Rule 5 and Regulation 9, Rule 2. Therefore, the 
odor control system will have an outlet H 2S concentration limit of 0.76 ppm. An outlet ammonia 
limit of 1 ppm will also be imposed on the odor control system.

 The facility proposed a VOC outlet concentration limit of 20 ppm. However, the odor control 
system is expected to emit more than 10 lb/day of VOC at that outlet concentration. VOC BACT 
for POTW odor control devices has been determined to be an outlet concentration of 10 ppm 
VOC, measured as methane, which will be imposed as a limit.    
 
The existing odor control system for Pretreatment Operations, S-110, consists of a dry media 
scrubber treating 18,000 cfm of foul air from the SEP 012 process area and two sea-shell 
biofilters, A-1204 and A-1205, treating a total of 5,000 cfm of foul air from the SEP 012 
distribution chambers and channel.   
 
 
EMISSIONS CALCULATIONS The new pretreatment facility, SEP 020 Headworks, will be enclosed. All emissions will be 
routed through A-110 and A-111, which are expected to remove 99% and 99.5% of inlet H 2S, 
respectively. However, the facility has asked for a limit of 1 ppm H 2S at the outlet and will 
receive a limit of 0.76 ppm to comply with Regulation 2, Rule 5 and Regulation 9, Rule 2. This 
limit is enforced to ensure that odors from the source are adequately controlled. Calculations 
were made using the Ideal Gas Law and assuming 1 ppmv H 2S and standard conditions at the 
abatement device outlets.  
 
Normal operation 
 
Normal operation of the new odor control system will take place whenever there are no carbon 
adsorption vessels being taken down for maintenance. In normal operation, foul air from S-110 
will exit through the three stacks associated with A-111 at a combined 91,000 cfm after passing 
through A-110. As bypass operation will occur for approximately 500 hours per year, the system 
will be in normal operation for approximately 8,260 hours per year.

Alternative Normal Operation 
 
Alternative normal operation will allow the system to emit all 91,000 cfm of treated foul air flow 
from the bypass stack, P-109, without first passing through A-111 to preserve the useful life of 
the carbon vessels. A-110 will be held to the same limits during alternative normal mode as 
during normal mode, so it will not cause an increase in emissions from what is described above. 
Emissions would only shift from A-111 to A-110 depending on the extent to which the system 
operates in alternative normal mode. 
 
Bypass mode 
 
Bypass mode operation of the new odor control system will take place whenever a carbon 
adsorption vessel needs to be taken down for maintenance. In bypass operation, 30,333 cfm of 
foul air from S-110 will exit through the bypass stack after passing through only A-110. The 
remaining 60,667 cfm will pass through the two A-111 stacks not down for maintenance after 
passing through A-110. Bypass operation is expected to occur for a maximum of 500 hours per 
year.

In addition, alternative normal operation will not increase emissions over normal operation and 
will increase the stack velocity at the bypass stack, P-109. The emission limits will remain the 
same and the flow rate through P-109 will increase from 30,333 cfm (during bypass mode for 
maintenance) to 91,000 cfm during alternative normal mode. This would increase dispersion of 
the treated foul air and lower the fence line H 2S concentration and health risk compared to the 
modeled scenarios.

A-110 and A-111 are going to have an organic emissions limit of 10 ppm, measured as methane, 
and will therefore satisfy BACT for POC.  
 
 
OFFSETS Emission offset requirements for POC and NOx are set out in Regulation 2, Rule 2, Section 302. 
Per a District policy, “Calculating Potential to Emit for Emergency Backup Power Generators”, 
signed on 6/3/2019, 100 hours of emergency operation will be assumed, in addition to the 50 
hours allowed for reliability-related and testing operation, for the purpose of determining the 
applicability of District permitting regulations. Therefore, this policy will be used in the 
calculation of the PTE of emergency backup power generators. POC and NOx offsets are 
required for new or modified sources at a facility that emits or will be permitted to emit 10 tons 
per year or more of that pollutant. The PTE for this facility is over 10 tpy for NOx and POC, so 
offsets are required. To date, offsets have been provided by the Small Facilities Bank. 
 
An analysis produced for the facility’s last application, #30297, in 2020, showed that the facility 
had the potential to emit 32.622 tons per year of NOx and 28.862 tons per year of POC.  
 
In this application, the facility will be changing the PTE by removing the existing headworks 
odor control system (A-1204, A1205) and installing the new odor control system abating SEP 
020 Headworks. The new PTE for NOx and POC would be 32.622 and 36.278 tons per year, 
respectively. The facility has agreed to lower the limit on the POC emission factor for S-10, 
Cogeneration Engine, in permit condition 17161, part 3 from 0.6 g POC/bhp-hr to 0.45 g/bhp-hr. 
This will lower the PTE for POC to 32.034 tons per year. The facility can continue to use offsets 
from the Small Facility Banking Account (SFBA) for NOx and POC. 
 
The offsets requirements for PM 10, PM2.5, and SOx are specified in Regulation 2, Rule 2, Section 
303. Per Section 303, PM 10, PM2.5, and SOx emission offsets are required for any new or 
modified source that is a major facility for PM 10, PM2.5, or SOx emissions. SFPUC is not a major 
facility for PM 10, PM2.5, and SOx emissions. Therefore, offsets for PM 10, PM2.5, and SOx are not 
required for this application.  
 
Offsets are not required for CO. 
 
The potential to emit calculations are in the file.
